comment_text.sn1.34:2.4: The deity seems to be expressing the non-Buddhist view that, while sensual pleasures in this realm are impermanent, the sinner goes to the realm of death from there is no return.
comment_text.sn1.34:3.1: These lines do not appear to be verse. The text marks them as a different speaker, but the identity of the speaker is unclear.
comment_text.sn1.34:7.2: Best known for his verses in the Way to the Far Shore (Snp 5.16), Mogharāja also features in Thag 2.44, where he lives outdoors, and AN 1.234:1.1 where he wears coarse robes. His appearance here is abrupt.

root_text.sn1.34:1.1: Ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā sāvatthiyaṁ viharati jetavane anāthapiṇḍikassa ārāme.
root_text.sn1.34:1.2: Atha kho sambahulā satullapakāyikā devatāyo abhikkantāya rattiyā abhikkantavaṇṇā kevalakappaṁ jetavanaṁ obhāsetvā yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkamiṁsu; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṁ abhivādetvā ekamantaṁ aṭṭhaṁsu.
root_text.sn1.34:1.3: Ekamantaṁ ṭhitā kho ekā devatā bhagavato santike imaṁ gāthaṁ abhāsi:
root_text.sn1.34:6.5: Devā manussā idha vā huraṁ vā,
root_text.sn1.34:7.3: Devā manussā idha vā huraṁ vā;

translation_text.sn1.34:0.2: 4. The Host of a Hundred Felicities
translation_text.sn1.34:1.1: At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s Monastery.
translation_text.sn1.34:1.2: Then, late at night, several glorious deities of the host of a hundred felicities, lighting up the entire Jeta’s Grove, went up to the Buddha, bowed, and stood to one side.
translation_text.sn1.34:1.3: Standing to one side, one deity spoke this verse in the Buddha’s presence:
translation_text.sn1.34:2.1: “Among mankind there are no sensual pleasures that are permanent.
translation_text.sn1.34:2.2: Here there are sensuous things, bound to which,
translation_text.sn1.34:2.3: drunk on which, there’s no coming back.
translation_text.sn1.34:2.4: That person doesn’t return from Death’s dominion.”
translation_text.sn1.34:3.1: “Gloom is born of desire; suffering is born of desire;
translation_text.sn1.34:3.2: when desire is removed, gloom is removed;
translation_text.sn1.34:3.3: when gloom is removed, suffering is removed.”
translation_text.sn1.34:4.1: “The world’s pretty things aren’t sensual pleasures.
translation_text.sn1.34:4.2: Greedy intention is a person’s sensual pleasure.
translation_text.sn1.34:4.3: The world’s pretty things stay just as they are,
translation_text.sn1.34:4.4: but the attentive remove desire for them.
translation_text.sn1.34:5.1: Give up anger, get rid of conceit,
translation_text.sn1.34:5.2: and get past all the fetters.
translation_text.sn1.34:5.3: Sufferings don’t torment the one who has nothing,
translation_text.sn1.34:5.4: not clinging to name and form.
translation_text.sn1.34:6.1: They gave up judgement without falling into conceit;
translation_text.sn1.34:6.2: craving for name and form was cut off right here.
translation_text.sn1.34:6.3: They cut the ties, untroubled, <j>with no need for hope.
translation_text.sn1.34:6.4: Though gods and humans search for them
translation_text.sn1.34:6.5: in this life or the next, they never find them,
translation_text.sn1.34:6.6: not in heaven nor in any abode.”
translation_text.sn1.34:7.1: “If neither gods nor humans see one freed in this way,”
translation_text.sn1.34:7.4: are those who revere that supreme man,
translation_text.sn1.34:7.5: who lives for the good of mankind, <j>also worthy of praise?”
translation_text.sn1.34:8.1: “The mendicants who revere one freed in this way,”
translation_text.sn1.34:8.4: Having understood the teaching <j>and given up doubt,

variant_text.sn1.34:6.1: na vimānamajjhagā → na ca mānamajjhagā (bj); na vimānamāgā (sya-all, km, pts2ed)
